data.body:
[G4 child-consistency audit — parent 72d5 (Migrate plan-review + completion-verification onto the workflow engine) × THIS child B1 (723c: Production batch-runner — extract sizing.py behind epic A's BatchRunner seam)]

Walked all 7 cross-child interaction modes for B1 against the parent + the WHOLE sibling roster (0408, 101a/B4, 5506/B-DM, 6b06/B2, 7e48/B6, 92fa/B-UX, 961b/B-RETIRE, dc58/B3, f12d/B5). NO high-confidence findings — clean pairing. Benign-reading rationale per mode below.

──────────────────────────────────────────────
MODES — all CLEAN (no finding)
──────────────────────────────────────────────

(1) Implicit shared state — CLEAN. B1 part-1 extracted the Pass-1 finder machinery from orchestrator.py into a NEW plan_review/pass1.py (run_pass1 + material_fingerprint + CONTAINER_CRITERIA helpers), re-exporting material_fingerprint for attest.py/__init__.py. This is a live-gate-touching refactor, but it was verified byte-for-byte behaviour-preserving (106 plan-review tests identical green before/after; subagent confirmed zero-diff run_pass1 body). The extracted run_pass1 is then SHARED by the bespoke path (orchestrator._run_passes) and the workflow path (ProductionBatchRunner) — and B-RETIRE (961b) EXPLICITLY keeps pass1.run_pass1 / sizing / ProductionBatchRunner alive when it removes the bespoke run_review. So the shared surface is acknowledged and sequenced across siblings, not hidden concurrent mutation. No write-write race or stale-read.

(2) Conflicting assumptions — CLEAN. B1's seam design (D3, batch-runner-seam.md:149-157) resolves the impedance mismatch: the engine's PlannedTraceRunner is an AgentStepRunner, but Pass-1 drives a rebar.llm.Runner directly, so the production runner OWNS an injectable rebar.llm.Runner and the seam's agent_runner is intentionally UNUSED. Every consuming sibling shares this assumption: B2's batch step (6b06) drives the runner with ProductionBatchRunner(runner=finder); B4's parity harness (101a) captures at the rebar.llm.Runner (TracingFakeRunner) seam for finders; B5 (f12d) dispatches verdict production through that same runner. No sibling assumes the agent_runner carries the finder calls. The opaque-plan / interpreter-never-branches invariant B1 journals is the same invariant epic A established and B2's interpreter relies on. Consistent.

(3) Dependency gap — CLEAN. B1 depends on epic A's BatchRunner/BatchRunRequest/BatchRunResult seam (cross-epic dep on 5766, present at the parent). B1 produces the runner B2 consumes — dep 723c blocks 6b06 is present and declared. No missing producer/consumer link in the roster.

(4) Scope overlap — CLEAN. B1 owns the adapter + the pass1.py extraction; AC3 pins sizing.py as the SINGLE home of the sizing algorithm (no duplication) and the new module as a thin adapter — verified (production_batch_runner.py contains no ladder/budget/shed algorithm; all flows through run_pass1 → sizing.*). B2 owns the workflow YAML authoring; B4 reads the runner's trace; B5 layers cutover. Each is a distinct layer over B1's artifact, not duplicated ownership. The catalog-wiring de-risk note on the parent (Joe, packaged plan-review criterion prompts must be added to the workflow catalog so a batch.criteria[].prompt lints clean) is a B2/B-DM surface, NOT B1's: B1's batch step uses plan-review-finder as the system prompt (req.finder) and resolves criterion descriptors via registry.by_id(), not catalog-lint at this level. No overlap with B1.

(5) Ordering violation — CLEAN. Pipeline order B1 → B2 → B4 → B5 → B-RETIRE is consistent with the declared deps (723c blocks 6b06; 6b06 blocks 101a; 101a blocks f12d; f12d blocks 961b). B1's own internal sequencing (part-1 behaviour-preserving extraction PR landed BEFORE part-2 runner, isolating the risky live-gate change) is sound. No ordering gap.

(6) Consumer impact — CLEAN. B1 is explicitly additive/offline: "No change to the bespoke orchestrator's behaviour yet (cutover is B5); this only makes the production runner available behind the seam." The pass1.py extraction is behaviour-preserving (zero-diff, all tests green), so the live claim gate is untouched at B1's boundary. The live cutover (and any consumer-facing change) is a B5 artifact, not B1's. No consumer break.

(7) Residual references — CLEAN. B1 references sizing.py, registry.py, det_floor/passes (via run_pass1), and the epic-A seam — all of which B-RETIRE (961b) explicitly PRESERVES ("Keep the SHARED machinery the workflow still uses: pass1.run_pass1, det_floor, registry, passes, sizing, attest, ProductionBatchRunner"). B6 (7e48) removes only examples/code_review.yaml + templates.py, which B1 does not touch. material_fingerprint re-export keeps attest.py/__init__.py consumers wired. No dangling/orphaned reference.

──────────────────────────────────────────────
ANTI-FP note: B1 is a thin, offline, behaviour-preserving adapter whose seam contract, ownership boundary, dependency links, and ordering are all consistent with — and explicitly referenced by — its consuming siblings (B2/B4/B5) and its downstream retirement story (B-RETIRE). The known epic-level live-plumbing defect (the {{plan}} variable gap surfaced on B2/0408/B5) is OFF B1's boundary: B1 supplies only the FINDER batch, whose prompt variables come from passes.pass1_chunk, not the verify/coach steps that lack {{plan}}. No high-confidence cross-child inconsistency attributable to this pairing. Findings list: EMPTY.
